questions.prompt: Why avoid sequential integer IDs in public API URLs?

choices.text: Enumeration risk — sequential IDs reveal information attackers can exploit. `/orders/124` and `/orders/125` exist; trying `/orders/123` reveals there ARE only ~125 orders (volume leakage). If access control is buggy (IDOR), enumeration is trivial — iterate IDs until you find one you can read. Even with proper auth, sequential IDs reveal business metrics: 'this company processes 1000 orders/day,' a competitor's lever. Solution: UUIDs (random 128-bit), Snowflake IDs (sortable but not predictable), nanoid (short random URL-safe). Stripe uses prefixed random IDs (`cus_abc123def`, `ord_xyz789`); the prefix is debuggable, the random portion is unguessable. Cost is small (longer URLs); benefit is removed information leakage + harder enumeration. Standard practice for any public-facing API in 2026; opaque IDs are simply the default.

questions.explanation: Sequential IDs leak volume info + enable enumeration. Use UUIDs or random IDs (Stripe-style).

choices.text: Too deep — 5 levels of nesting. Problems: long URLs (hard for humans to type and debug), multiple paths to same resource (`/users/123/orders/456/items/789` vs `/items/789` — which is canonical?), bracket complexity in client code, cognitive load reading the URL. Practical limit: 2-3 levels of nesting. Beyond that, flatten — comments belong to items belong to orders belong to users, but the URL doesn't need to capture all of that. Better: `/items/789/comments` if items are top-level (with `?order_id=456` filter if needed), or `/comments?item_id=789`. Resource hierarchy should mirror genuine 'always belongs to' relationships, not every dimension of identity. When you find yourself needing 4+ levels, you usually need to elevate some resources to top-level and use query params for relationships. The deep nesting smell is real; trust the discomfort and refactor.

questions.explanation: Too deep — 2-3 levels max. Elevate to top-level + query params for relationships at depth.
